---
title: "Direct Proof"
---

## Role

Direct proof is the baseline method: start from assumptions, use definitions and valid steps, and reach the conclusion.

## Intuition

A direct proof is the cleanest method when the structure of the claim already points forward.

## Basic Template

1. state the assumptions
2. unpack the relevant definitions
3. transform the expressions or objects in small justified steps
4. end exactly at the desired conclusion

## Worked Example

::: {#prp-divisible-six}
## Proposition

If an integer $n$ is divisible by $6$, then it is divisible by $3$.
:::

::: {.proof}
If $n$ is divisible by $6$, then $n = 6k$ for some integer $k$. Since $6k = 3(2k)$, and $2k$ is an integer, it follows that $n$ is divisible by $3$.
:::

## Why This Works

The proof succeeds because the definition of divisibility gives an algebraic representation, and that representation can be rearranged into the target form.

## Application Lens

Direct proof patterns show up everywhere:

- proving closure properties
- checking algorithm invariants
- verifying matrix identities
- establishing elementary bounds before a bigger theorem

## Paper Bridge

Even advanced proofs contain direct-proof fragments. When reading a hard paper, spotting those easier fragments can keep you oriented.
